name: Symmetrical Residential Wall Unit — Classical-Transitional with Stepped Crown Cornice (Element A) and Outer Tower Pilaster Strips (Element C)
description: A large, floor-standing, symmetrical residential wall cabinet conceived in a classical-transitional style. The client describes the design as resembling 'buildings on left and right,' underscoring a strongly architectural character — the unit is designed to command an entire wall and function as a room's dominant focal feature, not merely furniture pushed against a wall. The composition is organized into five distinct vertical zones, reading left to right: (1) LEFT FLANKING TOWER — a narrow full-height cabinet divided into exactly two zones by one horizontal shelf line: an upper open shelf area and a lower hinged door panel (marked '?' in the sketch, most likely a door). The outermost left edge carries Element C. (2) LEFT MAIN OPEN DISPLAY SECTION — the piece's dominant interior visual texture: a dense, irregular, staggered grid of open display niches in the upper area (approximately three columns of deliberately unequal widths × two to three staggered rows, creating an organic, puzzle-like pattern of open compartments), transitioning to a wider open shelf bay below the niche grid, and a likely closed panel at the lower base marked '?'. (3) CENTER SECTION — UPPER (above the bridge shelf): a pair of tall, completely plain hinged door panels divided by a thin center vertical seam, each panel bearing an elongated vertical bar pull near the seam; these panels are entirely featureless in deliberate contrast with the richly subdivided flanking niche zones. LOWER (below the bridge shelf): a six-drawer bank in a two-column × three-row arrangement divided by a visible center vertical stile, each of the six fronts bearing a centered wave or bar pull handle. The bridge shelf, a prominent horizontal surface spanning the center section at approximately counter height, is the primary structural and visual datum separating the upper display zone from the lower storage zone implied across the full unit width. (4) RIGHT MAIN OPEN DISPLAY SECTION — exact bilateral mirror of zone (2). (5) RIGHT FLANKING TOWER — exact bilateral mirror of zone (1), with Element C on the outer right edge. All five zones are crowned by Element A — a stepped crown cornice spanning the full unit width, rising in two to three distinct tiers from the outer tower crown level (lowest) inward to the center peak (highest). A shallow horizontal transom band of small closed compartments directly below the cornice and above the main upper zone was observed by two of three council members and is included at low confidence, pending client confirmation before fabrication. All surfaces are finished uniformly in smooth light beige painted wood.

dimensions.notes: Overall dimensions not specified by client. The sole client-stated dimension is Element C (outer pilaster strip width) at approximately 6 cm. All other proportions are estimates derived from sketch geometry and standard cabinetry practice only — they must not be treated as confirmed values and must not appear as specific numbers in any generated drawing. Relative proportional descriptions only.

style.aesthetic: Classical-Transitional — formal bilateral symmetry; stepped pediment crown (Element A) as architectural roofline rising to a center peak; outer pilasters with ornamental X-block capital and base (Element C) referencing classical column proportioning; contemporary irregular open-niche interior texture in organic contrast with the formal architectural exterior
influences: Neoclassical European cabinetry and stepped architectural pediment composition
Classical column and pilaster proportioning — capital, shaft, base (referenced by Element C)
Traditional symmetrical English library and reception-room casework
Contemporary transitional residential display shelving with irregular staggered cubby grids

materials.name: Painted MDF or solid wood panel construction
materials.usage: All carcass panels, shelving, drawer fronts, door panels, tower walls, cornice build-up layers, pilaster strips, transom band panels if confirmed
materials.finish: Smooth opaque light beige paint — uniform across ALL surfaces, interior and exterior, without exception

materials.name: Structural back panels (plywood or HDF)
materials.usage: Full-height, full-width back panel spanning all five zones — mandatory anti-racking structural element; interior face visible through all open shelves and niches
materials.finish: Light beige painted on interior face for cohesive open-display appearance

materials.name: Full-extension concealed soft-close drawer slides
materials.usage: All six drawers in center lower section (two-column × three-row bank)
materials.finish: Concealed metal runners — not visible in finished state

materials.name: Soft-close concealed cup hinges (35mm)
materials.usage: All hinged door panels throughout: center upper double doors, tower lower doors, flanking lower cabinet panels, transom band doors if confirmed by client
materials.finish: Concealed metal, overlay mounting — not visible in finished state

materials.name: Wave or bar pull handles
materials.usage: One centered pull per drawer front (six total); elongated vertical bar pulls on the two center upper door panels; consistent pull hardware on all other door panels across the unit
materials.finish: Client has not specified finish — monochromatic light beige paint or brushed metal accent are both candidates; to be confirmed with client

materials.name: Edge banding (PVC or solid wood)
materials.usage: All exposed front edges throughout: open shelf fronts, cornice step horizontal and vertical faces, door panel perimeters, drawer front edges, bridge shelf front edge, pilaster strip edges
materials.finish: Light beige painted to match carcass; no raw MDF edges permitted anywhere on the finished piece

colors.notes: Explicitly stated by client: 'light beige painted wood.' Monochromatic scheme — single uniform color across ALL surfaces: exterior carcass panels, interior shelf faces, open niche side walls, cornice step faces, pilaster strips, drawer fronts, door panels, transom band, plinth. HIGH confidence. The sketch is a monochrome line drawing; all color information is derived entirely from client text. Approximate equivalents: RAL 1015 Light Ivory, RAL 1013 Oyster White, or Farrow and Ball 'Pointing' — a warm off-white pale beige.

structural_elements.name: Element A — Stepped Crown Cornice
structural_elements.description: The defining top silhouette of the entire unit — the architectural roofline in the client's own metaphor. The top profile is DEFINITIVELY NOT FLAT. The cornice rises in two to three distinct horizontal step tiers from the outer tower crown level (the lowest point, at each flanking tower top) inward and upward to the center peak (the highest point of the entire unit). Each tier forms a true three-dimensional ledge with a horizontal top face and a vertical riser face — both visible in front elevation. The stepped profile spans the full unit width across all five zones. Step direction is fixed: outer edges are lowest; center peak is highest. Where the lowest cornice tier meets the top of each Element C pilaster, the X-block capital ornament of the pilaster aligns with and visually anchors that corner junction. The exact step tier count (two versus three) is approximate due to sketch resolution; the existence and direction of the stepped non-flat profile are certain.
structural_elements.material: Built-up MDF laminations or applied solid-wood molding sections forming the step profile; all faces and edges painted light beige
structural_elements.position: Full-width top of entire unit spanning all five zones; height varies from the lowest tier at each outer tower crown, rising to the highest tier at the center peak

structural_elements.name: Element C — Outer Tower Pilaster Strips
structural_elements.description: Two narrow vertical pilaster strips at the outermost front edges of each flanking tower, forming the leftmost and rightmost visible boundaries of the entire unit in any front-facing view. Each pilaster is approximately 6 cm wide — the only dimension explicitly stated by the client. Each strip has three distinct parts: (a) a distinctively shaped ornamental CAPITAL BLOCK at the very top, bearing X or cross-pattern marks engraved or applied into the face; (b) a plain, flat, unadorned vertical shaft connecting the two blocks, with no decorative treatment; (c) a matching ornamental BASE BLOCK at the very bottom, identical in character and X-pattern marking to the capital block. This capital-shaft-base arrangement directly references classical column proportioning and is fully consistent with the client's 'buildings' metaphor. Both pilasters are mandatory and must appear in every front-facing view.
structural_elements.material: Solid wood or MDF built up to the required width; X-pattern block relief at top and bottom achieved by routing, applied molding, or applied carved solid-wood elements; painted light beige
structural_elements.position: Outermost front edge of left flanking tower (leftmost element in front elevation) and outermost front edge of right flanking tower (rightmost element); full tower height from base block at floor level to capital block at cornice junction

structural_elements.name: Left Flanking Tower
structural_elements.description: Narrow full-height vertical cabinet module just inside the left Element C pilaster strip. Divided into exactly two zones by ONE visible horizontal shelf line: UPPER ZONE — open shelf area with one to two shelf levels (sketch annotations suggest two levels); LOWER ZONE — a single hinged door panel spanning the full interior tower width, marked '?' in the sketch. Default interpretation: hinged cabinet door consistent with the overall lower-zone closed-storage theme; confirm with client before fabrication. Zone proportions: the upper zone occupies approximately the upper half to two-thirds of the tower's total interior height; the lower zone occupies the remaining lower portion. The two-zone reading is the majority council position (Claude and GPT); a three-zone alternative (top door, middle open, bottom door) was raised by one council member and should also be confirmed with the client.
structural_elements.material: 18mm MDF carcass and door panel; concealed soft-close hinge on lower door; painted light beige
structural_elements.position: Far left end of unit, full height from base plinth top to cornice underside at the lowest step tier, directly inside the left Element C pilaster strip

structural_elements.name: Right Flanking Tower
structural_elements.description: Exact bilateral mirror of the Left Flanking Tower in every respect: same two-zone structure (upper open shelf area plus lower hinged door panel '?'), same proportions, same material specification, same hardware. Element C pilaster strip on the outer right edge. The mirror must be geometrically precise.
structural_elements.material: Same specification as Left Flanking Tower
structural_elements.position: Far right end of unit, full height, directly inside the right Element C pilaster strip

structural_elements.name: Left Main Open Display Section
structural_elements.description: The piece's dominant interior visual focal zone, second from left, spanning from the right boundary of the left tower to the left boundary of the center section, full height. The section divides into an UPPER DISPLAY AREA and a LOWER BASE AREA at the same implied horizontal datum as the bridge shelf in the center section. UPPER DISPLAY AREA (approximately two-thirds of the section's total height): an irregular, staggered grid of open display niches — CRITICAL: approximately three columns of DELIBERATELY UNEQUAL widths (some narrow, some wider, a non-uniform, organic rhythm — not a uniform grid) × approximately two to three rows per column, with shelf heights staggered so they do not align uniformly across columns. This yields approximately six to nine individual open compartments of varying size. Faint internal depth lines in each compartment indicate the void recedes into the cabinet. A wider open shelf bay sits below the niche grid within the upper area. LOWER BASE AREA (approximately one-third of total section height, at the same height zone as the center drawer bank and tower lower doors): a closed panel area marked '?' — most likely a hinged door panel; client to confirm. This entire section is the most carefully and densely drawn zone in the original sketch.
structural_elements.material: 18mm MDF carcass panels; minimum 25mm shelf thickness for long spans; adjustable shelf pin system; painted light beige
structural_elements.position: Second zone from left, between left flanking tower and center section; full height

structural_elements.name: Right Main Open Display Section
structural_elements.description: Exact bilateral mirror of the Left Main Open Display Section in every respect: identical staggered irregular open niche grid in the upper display area (column widths and staggered shelf heights reflected precisely, not independently recreated), identical wider open shelf bay below niches, identical '?' lower base panel. The mirror must be geometrically exact — this section is the clearest test of the absolute bilateral symmetry that defines the piece.
structural_elements.material: Same specification as Left Main Open Display Section
structural_elements.position: Second zone from right, between center section and right flanking tower; full height

structural_elements.name: Center Upper Double Door Panels
structural_elements.description: A pair of tall, completely plain hinged door panels occupying the full upper zone of the center section, from the bridge shelf surface below to the top of the upper zone (the transom band base if confirmed, or the cornice underside if the transom band is absent). The two panels meet at a thin center vertical seam or stile dividing the center section width exactly in half. Each door panel face is entirely plain and blank — NO internal shelf lines, no divisions, no decorative elements of any kind — creating a strong visual contrast with the richly subdivided flanking niche sections. Each panel bears one elongated vertical bar or rod pull handle positioned near and parallel to the center seam. All three council members independently concluded these are closed door panels; this is the highest-confidence element in the center section. These are most likely the 'doors that can be clearly seen' referenced by the client.
structural_elements.material: 18mm MDF door panels; concealed soft-close 35mm cup hinges; elongated vertical bar pull hardware; painted light beige
structural_elements.position: Upper zone of center section; sitting on the bridge shelf top surface; top edges at the transom band base or cornice underside

structural_elements.name: Bridge Shelf and Counter Surface
structural_elements.description: A prominent horizontal surface spanning the full center section width at approximately counter height — roughly mid-height of the overall unit, dividing the center section into its upper door zone and its lower drawer zone. Clearly drawn in the sketch as a distinct horizontal element with a visible front-face edge (confirming it as a physical surface with thickness and not merely a structural divider line). Functions simultaneously as: the structural floor of the upper double door zone; the structural ceiling of the lower six-drawer bank; and a usable counter or display surface. The height of this shelf establishes the primary horizontal datum of the entire composition — this same height is the implied boundary between the upper display zone and the lower storage zone across all five sections of the unit.
structural_elements.material: Minimum 25mm MDF horizontal panel for span load capacity; solid-wood or PVC T-profile edge banding on the visible front face edge; painted light beige
structural_elements.position: Mid-height of center section only, spanning the full center section width; the front edge is visible in elevation as a horizontal band with thickness at approximately counter height from the finished floor

structural_elements.name: Center Lower Six-Drawer Bank
structural_elements.description: Six individual drawer fronts arranged in a two-column × three-row matrix occupying the lower zone of the center section, below the bridge shelf. A visible center vertical stile runs the full height of the lower center zone, dividing it into a LEFT COLUMN of three stacked drawer fronts and a RIGHT COLUMN of three stacked drawer fronts (three rows per column; six fronts total). Each of the six drawer fronts bears one centered wave-style or bar pull handle, visible as a wavy or straight short horizontal mark on each front (the '~' tilde symbol in the original sketch). CRITICAL INSTRUCTION: this is NOT three single full-width drawers. The center vertical stile is a confirmed and visually present element — all three council members agreed on the two-column × three-row (six drawer) arrangement. Full-extension soft-close concealed drawer slides throughout.
structural_elements.material: 18mm MDF drawer boxes and overlay drawer fronts; full-extension soft-close concealed metal slides; wave or bar pull hardware; painted light beige
structural_elements.position: Lower zone of center section, below the bridge shelf, between the left and right flanking lower cabinet panels of the main display sections

structural_elements.name: Flanking Lower Cabinet Panels
structural_elements.description: Two closed cabinet areas forming the lower bases of the left and right main display sections, at the same height zone as the center six-drawer bank (from the floor plinth to the bridge shelf datum height). One panel on each side — the left flanking panel is the lower base of the left main display section and sits immediately to the left of the center drawer bank; the right flanking panel is the lower base of the right main display section and sits immediately to the right of the center drawer bank. Together with the tower lower doors and the central drawer bank, these panels form the complete closed lower storage band across the full unit width. Both panels are marked '?' in the original sketch. Most likely treatment: hinged cabinet doors consistent with the overall closed lower-storage theme. Must be geometrically symmetrical. Client to confirm treatment before fabrication.
structural_elements.material: 18mm MDF door or panel fronts; concealed soft-close hinges if confirmed as doors; consistent pull hardware; painted light beige
structural_elements.position: Lower zone only: left flanking panel below the left main display section (immediately left of center drawer bank); right flanking panel below the right main display section (immediately right of center drawer bank); both at the same height as the drawer bank and tower lower doors

structural_elements.name: Tower Lower Door Panels
structural_elements.description: A single hinged door panel in the lower zone of each flanking tower, spanning the full interior width of that tower. Part of the continuous closed lower storage band running across the full width of the unit. Marked '?' in the sketch at both tower lower zones. Default interpretation: hinged cabinet doors. Concealed soft-close hinges; pull hardware consistent with the rest of the unit.
structural_elements.material: 18mm MDF door panel; concealed soft-close 35mm hinges; pull hardware; painted light beige
structural_elements.position: Lower zone of the left flanking tower AND lower zone of the right flanking tower; height consistent with the overall lower zone datum established by the bridge shelf

structural_elements.name: Transom Upper Band
structural_elements.description: [LOW CONFIDENCE — PENDING CLIENT CONFIRMATION BEFORE FABRICATION] A shallow horizontal band of small closed compartments or paneled sections spanning the full interior width of the unit — between the inner faces of both tower walls — positioned directly below Element A (the stepped crown cornice underside) and directly above the top edges of the main display section upper niche grids and the center door panels. Two of three council members (GPT, revised in final round; Gemini, consistent throughout) independently observed a horizontal band with small enclosed rectangular fields in this position in the original sketch. One council member (Claude) held firmly that this element is absent and is a misreading of the cornice transition zone. If present: a row of small, equally proportioned closed-front compartments of shallow height forming a continuous upper storage frieze across the interior width. If absent: the cornice underside connects directly to the tops of the niche grids and center door panels with no intervening band. Do not fabricate without explicit client confirmation. Render in all drawings and annotate clearly.
structural_elements.position: Full interior width span between tower inner walls, directly below Element A cornice underside, above the main upper display and door zone top edges

structural_elements.name: Base Plinth
structural_elements.description: Full-width recessed kick-plate plinth at floor level running under the entire unit footprint. Provides a clean transition from floor to the cabinet face, grounds the piece architecturally, and protects lower door and panel edges from floor-level damage. The plinth is slightly set back from the main front face plane, creating a shallow reveal at the base. Painted light beige. Explicit visibility of this element in the sketch is limited — it is included as a fabrication-standard structural requirement for a unit of this scale.
structural_elements.position: Floor level, full unit width; set back slightly behind the front face plane

spatial_layout: Five-zone horizontal wall unit reading left to right: [Left Element C Pilaster Strip — approximately 6 cm, client-stated] | [Left Flanking Tower] | [Left Main Open Display Section] | [Center Section] | [Right Main Open Display Section] | [Right Flanking Tower] | [Right Element C Pilaster Strip — approximately 6 cm, client-stated]. The two tower zones are of roughly equal and relatively narrow width; the two main display sections are of roughly equal and wider width; the center section width is estimated from sketch proportions. All widths are relative — no confirmed numerical values except Element C at approximately 6 cm. Vertical organization from floor upward: [Base Plinth — shallow band at floor] | [Lower Storage Zone — a continuous horizontal band at consistent height across all five sections: Tower lower door panels at the far ends; Flanking lower cabinet panels below the main display sections; Center six-drawer bank (two columns × three rows) in the center] | [Bridge Shelf — prominent horizontal surface with visible front edge at approximately counter height in the center section only; its height marks the consistent dividing datum across the full unit] | [Upper Display and Door Zone — Tower upper open shelf areas near the outer ends; Staggered irregular open niche grids occupying the upper areas of the main display sections; Two tall plain door panels in the center upper zone] | [Transom Band — shallow closed band spanning full interior width, low confidence, pending confirmation] | [Element A Stepped Crown Cornice — full width, stepping from the outer tower crown level (lowest) up to the center unit peak (highest) in two to three tiers]. Absolute bilateral symmetry around the center vertical axis is the governing organizational principle for every element in every zone.

context.placement: Floor-standing, against a primary wall in a residential interior. The scale and architectural character of the piece strongly indicate a living room, formal reception room, drawing room, or study. The unit is conceived as the room's dominant architectural focal element — an entire wall composition, not merely furniture.
context.surroundings: Residential interior. Light beige finish is neutral and warm, compatible with a broad range of interior color palettes. The classical-transitional aesthetic is appropriate for formal or semi-formal living contexts. The client's 'buildings' metaphor indicates a desire for gravitas and architectural presence.
context.scale_reference: No human figure or reliable dimension lines are present in the sketch. The only client-stated dimension is Element C at approximately 6 cm. All other proportions are relative estimates from sketch geometry. Overall dimensions must not be specified without client-measured input.

views_to_generate.label: Floor Plan — Top-Down Cabinet Footprint
views_to_generate.description: Flat 2D top-down technical diagram of the cabinet footprint as seen from directly above. Camera pointing straight down. Shows the five-zone internal division, Element C pilaster strips at outer edges, internal zone dividers and shelf lines visible from above, door-opening gaps (straight gaps only, no arcs) along the front edge, and back panel indication. No specific dimensions except Element C approximately 6 cm.
views_to_generate.generation_prompt:
STEP 1 — OVERALL FORM:
This is a flat 2D technical top-down diagram of a large residential wall cabinet as seen from directly above — a camera pointing straight down, rendering nothing but what is visible from above. The footprint of the cabinet is a single wide, shallow rectangle, substantially wider than it is deep: the unit is far longer left-to-right than it is front-to-back. A rough sense of proportion: the width is approximately four to six times the depth. The overall outer perimeter is a clean rectangle with no projecting wings, steps, or curves (this is the default uniform-depth plan; a U-shaped stepped-depth alternative is architecturally plausible but unresolved — it must be annotated as an open question). Five internal vertical zones divide the width from left to right. All outer boundary lines are drawn as thick lines; all internal dividers, shelf lines, and annotations are drawn as thin lines on a white background.

STEP 2 — OUTER BOUNDARY:
Draw the four perimeter walls as thick lines forming the complete cabinet rectangle: (a) LEFT SIDE WALL — a short thick line running the full depth of the cabinet, front to back, at the far left; (b) RIGHT SIDE WALL — an identical short thick line at the far right; (c) FRONT FACE — the longest thick line spanning the full width at the near side (the face of the cabinet that is exposed to the room); (d) BACK PANEL — a parallel thick line the same full length at the rear. All four corners are right angles.

STEP 3 — ELEMENT C PILASTER STRIPS (leftmost and rightmost sub-zones):
Immediately inside the left side wall, draw a very narrow internal rectangle — approximately 6 cm wide in plan (the only client-stated dimension) — running the full front-to-back depth of the cabinet. This represents the left Element C pilaster strip as seen from above: a thin but definite band of material. Label it: "ELEM. C — PILASTER STRIP (~6 cm — ONLY CLIENT-CONFIRMED DIMENSION)." Mirror this exactly on the right side, immediately inside the right side wall. Label identically. These two narrow bands are the leftmost and rightmost internal rectangles in the entire floor plan.

STEP 4 — FIVE-ZONE INTERNAL DIVIDERS:
Inside the outer boundary and inside the Element C bands, draw four thin vertical lines running the full depth (front to back) to create the five internal cabinet zones. Working inward from the left after the Element C band: (1) a vertical line marking the right boundary of the Left Flanking Tower; (2) a vertical line marking the right boundary of the Left Main Display Section; (3) a vertical line marking the right boundary of the Center Section; (4) a vertical line marking the right boundary of the Right Main Display Section. The Right Flanking Tower fills the remaining space before the right Element C band. The two tower zones are narrow; the two main display sections are wider; the center section width is estimated from sketch proportions. All proportions are relative — no numerical values. Label each zone within its column with a short horizontal text label: "LEFT TOWER," "LEFT MAIN DISPLAY," "CENTER," "RIGHT MAIN DISPLAY," "RIGHT TOWER."

STEP 5 — BACK PANEL INDICATION:
Along the full rear boundary, draw a second thin line parallel to and just inside the thick rear boundary line. This inner thin line represents the front face of the structural back panel running across all five zones. Label this thin inner line: "STRUCTURAL BACK PANEL." The narrow gap between the thin inner line and the thick rear boundary represents the panel thickness.

STEP 6 — LEFT FLANKING TOWER (plan view detail):
Within the Left Tower zone, draw one thin line parallel to the front face edge running across the zone at the approximate shelf depth position within the upper zone — this represents the front edge of the tower's upper shelf as seen from above. In the lower zone of the tower (the door zone), at the front boundary line, draw a short straight gap in the front boundary thick line to indicate the tower lower door opening. Add a small arrow and label reading "DOOR OPENING — '?' (PENDING CLIENT CONF.)." No swing arcs or curves.

STEP 7 — LEFT MAIN OPEN DISPLAY SECTION (plan view detail):
Within the Left Main Display zone, draw approximately two thin vertical lines running front-to-back at unequal spacing within this zone — representing the column dividers of the upper niche grid as seen from above. The spacing between these vertical lines should be unequal (some gaps wider, some narrower) to reflect the deliberately non-uniform column widths of the staggered niche grid. Also draw one or two thin horizontal shelf lines (running left-to-right within this zone, parallel to the front face) at approximate depth positions representing visible shelf edges from above. In the lower base zone, at the front boundary line, draw a straight gap labeled "LOWER PANEL — '?' (PENDING CLIENT CONF.)."

STEP 8 — CENTER SECTION (plan view detail):
Within the Center zone, show several sub-elements visible from above: (a) BRIDGE SHELF FRONT EDGE — draw a thin horizontal line running left-to-right across the center zone at a position roughly mid-depth within the zone (the front edge of the bridge shelf is visible from above as this line); label it "BRIDGE SHELF FRONT EDGE." (b) UPPER CENTER DOORS — in the area between the bridge shelf line and the front boundary, draw a thin center vertical line splitting the upper center zone in half, representing the door seam; draw two short straight gaps in the front boundary line for the two door openings; label "DOUBLE DOORS (×2) — UPPER CENTER." (c) LOWER CENTER DRAWER BANK — in the area between the bridge shelf line and the rear area, draw one vertical line down the center (the center vertical stile) and three thin horizontal lines creating six small rectangles: the top-down view of the six-drawer bank occupying two columns and three rows; label "6-DRAWER BANK: 2 COL × 3 ROWS." (d) FLANKING LOWER PANELS — on the left and right sides of the lower center area, at the front boundary, draw straight gap openings and label each "LOWER PANEL '?' (PENDING CONF.)."

STEP 9 — RIGHT MAIN OPEN DISPLAY SECTION (plan view detail):
Exact mirror of Step 7. Identical unequal vertical column divider lines, mirrored left-to-right. Same shelf depth line positions. Same lower panel door gap at front boundary, labeled identically.

STEP 10 — RIGHT FLANKING TOWER (plan view detail):
Exact mirror of Step 6. Same shelf depth line from above. Same door opening gap at front boundary of lower zone, labeled identically.

STEP 11 — ELEMENT C DETAILS IN PLAN:
Within the narrow Element C pilaster bands at far left and far right, add a small square at the forward-most corner and the rear-most corners of each band (near the front face line and near the back panel line). These small squares represent the footprints of the capital block (at front) and the base block (at rear) — or alternatively, just the capital block is at the front-face edge; note this ambiguity with a label "X-BLOCK CAPITAL (front face)" on the forward corner mark.

STEP 12 — DEPTH CONFIGURATION ANNOTATION:
Add a prominent annotation box outside the cabinet boundary reading: "DEFAULT: UNIFORM DEPTH PLAN SHOWN. ALTERNATIVE: U-SHAPED STEPPED DEPTH (towers and lower base protrude deeper; upper center section recessed toward back wall) — architecturally plausible, raised by one council member. CONFIRM WITH CLIENT before finalizing the section profile."

STEP 13 — GENERAL NOTES AND LABELS:
Add the following annotations to the drawing: (a) "ALL SURFACES: LIGHT BEIGE PAINTED WOOD — UNIFORM, MONOCHROMATIC. NO CONTRAST ZONES." (b) "BILATERAL SYMMETRY AXIS" — draw a thin dashed vertical line at the exact horizontal center of the cabinet width and label it. (c) "ONLY CONFIRMED DIMENSION: ELEM. C ≈ 6 cm. ALL OTHER PROPORTIONS ESTIMATED FROM SKETCH ONLY." (d) A small legend box: "DOOR OPENINGS SHOWN AS STRAIGHT GAPS IN BOUNDARY — NO SWING ARCS."

STEP 14 — CRITICAL DOOR-OPENING RULE:
Verify that every door opening in the plan is shown exclusively as a straight gap in the front boundary thick line with no additional curves, arcs, or quarter-circle lines of any kind attached to it. A text label at each gap is the sole indicator of the door type and location.

CONCLUSION: The completed floor plan is a clean, wide, shallow rectangular diagram divided into five clearly labeled vertical zones with narrow pilaster bands at both outer edges, unequal internal column dividers in the display sections reflecting the staggered niche grid, a bridge shelf front-edge line in the center zone, six-drawer matrix lines in the lower center, straight-gap door indicators along the front edge in all door zones, and clear annotations of the only confirmed dimension (Element C approximately 6 cm) alongside chairman-level flags for the depth configuration and all pending client confirmations.

views_to_generate.label: Front Elevation — Primary Display Face
views_to_generate.description: Flat 2D frontal elevation of the full cabinet front face viewed straight on, perfectly perpendicularly. Shows all elements top to bottom across all five zones: Element A stepped crown cornice; transom band (annotated low confidence); Element C pilasters with X-block capitals and bases; tower two-zone structures; staggered irregular open niche grids in main sections; center double door panels above bridge shelf; six-drawer bank (two columns × three rows) and flanking lower panels below bridge shelf; base plinth at floor. Strictly bilaterally symmetrical around the center vertical axis.
views_to_generate.generation_prompt:
STEP 1 — OVERALL FORM:
This is a flat 2D technical front elevation drawing of the full cabinet face, viewed perfectly straight-on and perpendicularly — no perspective, no foreshortening, no depth visible, no sides shown. The drawing shows only the single front face. The overall silhouette is wide and commanding: the unit is substantially wider than it is tall at the tower level — roughly four to five times wider than the main tower height — but the stepped cornice (Element A) rises further above the center, making the center the tallest point of the composition. The TOP EDGE IS NOT A FLAT LINE — it is a stepped profile rising from both outer ends upward to a center peak. The LEFT and RIGHT boundaries are defined by narrow ornamental pilaster trim strips (Element C) with decorative blocks at their tops and bottoms. The visual impression of the completed elevation must be: a wide, authoritative, architecturally-scaled cabinet with a dramatic stepped crown and highly articulated interior shelving texture.

STEP 2 — ELEMENT C — LEFT AND RIGHT OUTER PILASTER STRIPS (establish the outer vertical boundaries first):
At the extreme left edge of the drawing, draw a narrow vertical rectangle representing the left Element C pilaster strip. This strip is approximately 6 cm wide — the only client-confirmed dimension — annotated accordingly. The strip runs the full height of the left flanking tower (from the base plinth top to the lowest cornice step level). At the VERY TOP of this strip, draw a clearly defined SQUARE ORNAMENTAL CAPITAL BLOCK: a small solid square with two diagonal lines crossing its face in an X or cross pattern, or a grid of lines creating a crosshatch relief pattern. This block is wider than the pilaster strip itself — it projects slightly left and right to read as a distinct capital. Below the capital block, the pilaster shaft is a plain, flat, unadorned vertical rectangle with no decorative marks whatsoever. At the VERY BOTTOM of this strip, draw a SQUARE ORNAMENTAL BASE BLOCK, identical in size, shape, and X-pattern marking to the capital block. Label both: "X-BLOCK CAPITAL" at the top and "X-BLOCK BASE" at the bottom. Label the strip: "ELEM. C — PILASTER (~6 cm — ONLY CLIENT-STATED DIM.)." Mirror this entire pilaster strip EXACTLY at the extreme right edge for the right Element C pilaster. Both pilasters are the absolute leftmost and rightmost elements in the entire elevation.

STEP 3 — ELEMENT A — STEPPED CROWN CORNICE (establish the top silhouette across the full width):
Spanning the full unit width from the top of the left pilaster capital to the top of the right pilaster capital, draw the stepped crown cornice top profile. Begin at the far left: start at the level of the top of the left flanking tower (this is the LOWEST tier of the cornice, the tower crown level — call it the "baseline level" of the cornice). From this baseline level at the left outer edge, step upward inward: draw a short horizontal line inward (the top face of the first cornice step), then a short vertical riser line upward, then another horizontal line inward (the top face of the second step), then another vertical riser upward. If a third step is included, repeat. Continue until reaching the center peak — the HIGHEST point of the entire cornice — at the center of the unit. Mirror this step profile exactly from the right outer edge rising to the same center peak. The center peak is where the leftward-rising and rightward-rising step profiles meet symmetrically. Each step has a clear, distinct horizontal top face and a clear vertical riser — they must be unambiguous three-dimensional ledges in the drawing, not softened curves. Label: "ELEM. A — STEPPED CROWN CORNICE. Center is the highest point. Tiers rise from outer edges inward and upward. 2–3 tiers — exact count to be confirmed with client."

STEP 4 — TRANSOM UPPER BAND (directly below the cornice, full interior width — LOW CONFIDENCE):
Directly below the underside of Element A, spanning the full interior width of the unit (from the inner edge of the left tower to the inner edge of the right tower), draw a shallow horizontal band. Inside this band, draw a row of small equally proportioned closed rectangular compartments side by side across the full width. The band height is shallow — approximately one-quarter to one-fifth the height of the main upper display zone below it. Each compartment in the row is a simple small flat rectangle. The compartments abut on both sides the inner walls of the two towers. Draw a prominent annotation box pointing to this band reading: "TRANSOM BAND — OBSERVED BY 2 OF 3 COUNCIL MEMBERS. LOW CONFIDENCE. DISPUTED BY 1 COUNCIL MEMBER. DO NOT FABRICATE WITHOUT CLIENT CONFIRMATION. If absent, cornice connects directly to tops of niche grids and center doors."

STEP 5 — LEFT FLANKING TOWER (narrow zone at far left, inside Element C pilaster):
The left tower occupies the zone immediately inside the left Element C pilaster strip, running the full height from the base plinth top to the underside of the lowest cornice tier (the tower crown level). The tower's left boundary is the inner edge of the Element C pilaster; the right boundary is the tower-to-main-display divider line. Draw ONE thin horizontal shelf line across the tower interior at approximately fifty to sixty-five percent of the tower height measured from the base plinth upward. This single shelf line divides the tower into exactly two zones.
UPPER TOWER ZONE (above the shelf line): an open shelving area. Draw one or two additional fine horizontal lines within this zone indicating shelf levels. The zone is open — no door outlines, no panel marks. The interiors between the shelf lines are visible open voids.
LOWER TOWER ZONE (below the shelf line, down to the plinth): a closed door panel. Draw the boundary rectangle of this zone as a plain flat closed face. Draw a small centered horizontal pull mark or short vertical bar on this face. Label: "TOWER LOWER DOOR — '?' (CONFIRM WITH CLIENT)."
Label the upper zone: "OPEN SHELVES." Label the full tower: "LEFT TOWER." Add a small annotation: "NOTE: 2-zone reading adopted (majority). 3-zone alternative (top door + middle open + bottom door) raised by Gemini — confirm with client."

STEP 6 — LEFT MAIN OPEN DISPLAY SECTION (second from left, full height):
The left main display section occupies the zone between the right boundary of the left tower and the left boundary of the center section, spanning the full height. This is the most complex and visually important zone in the entire drawing. It is divided horizontally into an UPPER DISPLAY AREA and a LOWER BASE AREA at the same height as the bridge shelf in the center section (this height is the consistent horizontal datum across the full width).
UPPER DISPLAY AREA (approximately the upper two-thirds of this section's height, from the lower base boundary up to the transom band or cornice): Draw the staggered open niche grid. This is CRITICAL — the column widths must be UNEQUAL. Create approximately three vertical divider lines within this section at deliberately UNEQUAL HORIZONTAL SPACING: for example, the leftmost column is narrow, the middle column is wide, and the rightmost column is narrower — or another non-uniform arrangement that clearly avoids any appearance of a uniform grid. This creates three columns of clearly different widths. Within EACH column, draw horizontal shelf lines at positions that are STAGGERED — the shelf lines in one column do NOT align with the shelf lines in the adjacent column. This creates an irregular, organic, puzzle-like pattern of open compartments of varying widths and heights. In several of the resulting open compartments, draw faint thin diagonal lines or parallel lines near the inside corners, suggesting that the void extends into the cabinet depth. Draw the annotation "2  2" (two numerals with space between, echoing the sketch) near the top of this section. Label: "STAGGERED OPEN NICHE GRID — COLUMN WIDTHS DELIBERATELY UNEQUAL. CRITICAL: Do NOT render as a uniform grid."
LOWER BASE AREA (approximately the lower one-third of this section's height, from the base plinth up to the horizontal datum line): Draw a plain closed rectangular face spanning the full width of this section at this lower height. Draw a centered small pull mark on this face. Label: "LOWER BASE — '?' PANEL (CONFIRM WITH CLIENT)."
Label the full section: "LEFT MAIN DISPLAY SECTION."

STEP 7 — CENTER SECTION (center zone, full height, divided by the bridge shelf):
The center section spans between the right boundary of the left main display section and the left boundary of the right main display section.

BRIDGE SHELF ELEMENT: At the horizontal datum height (approximately counter height — roughly the lower one-third of the total unit interior height, consistent with the top of the lower base areas of the flanking sections), draw a prominent horizontal band spanning the full center section width. This band consists of TWO closely-spaced horizontal lines: the upper line is the top surface of the bridge shelf; the lower line is the front face of the shelf edge. The gap between the two lines represents the visible thickness of the shelf — it should be clearly legible as a surface with physical presence, not merely a thin grid line. Label: "BRIDGE SHELF — COUNTER SURFACE. Front edge visible. Primary horizontal datum of full unit."

CENTER UPPER ZONE (above the bridge shelf, up to the transom band or cornice underside): Draw a single thin vertical line running the full height of this zone precisely at the horizontal center of the center section — this is the door seam dividing the two door panels. To the LEFT of this seam sits the LEFT DOOR PANEL: a plain, completely blank rectangle spanning from the seam to the left boundary of the center section and from the bridge shelf top surface up to the upper zone ceiling. NO internal lines, NO shelf edges, NO subdivisions of any kind inside this rectangle. Near the seam edge of the left door panel, draw one elongated thin vertical line or bar running approximately three-quarters of the door panel height, positioned within the panel near the seam — this is the elongated vertical pull handle. Match it on the RIGHT DOOR PANEL: an identical plain blank rectangle from the seam to the right boundary, same height, with one elongated vertical pull bar near the seam on its side. Label both: "CENTER UPPER — DOUBLE DOOR PANELS (×2). Plain blank faces — confirmed by all 3 council members."

CENTER LOWER ZONE (below the bridge shelf, down to the base plinth top): Draw one VERTICAL CENTER STILE — a thin but clear and definite vertical line running the full height of the lower center zone, precisely at the center of the center section. This stile divides the lower zone into LEFT COLUMN and RIGHT COLUMN. Within EACH column, draw TWO horizontal lines creating THREE equal horizontal bands — the three stacked drawer fronts per column. This produces a total of six drawer rectangles (three in the left column, three in the right column). In the CENTER of each of the six drawer front rectangles, draw a small wavy line or short horizontal bar to represent the wave or bar pull handle on each drawer front. All six drawer fronts must have a centered pull indicated. Label: "6-DRAWER BANK — 2 COLUMNS × 3 ROWS. Center vertical stile confirmed. NOT 3 full-width drawers."

STEP 8 — RIGHT MAIN OPEN DISPLAY SECTION (second from right, full height):
Exact geometric bilateral mirror of the Left Main Display Section. All elements are mirrored around the center vertical axis: the staggered niche grid column widths are reflected (so if the left section had narrow-wide-narrow reading left to right, the right section reads narrow-wide-narrow reading left to right — a true mirror), the staggered shelf heights within each column are reflected, the wider open bay below the niche grid is reflected. The lower base '?' panel is reflected. The resulting niche patterns, when seen together in the elevation, must look unmistakably like a left-to-right mirror image of one another. Label: "RIGHT MAIN DISPLAY — EXACT BILATERAL MIRROR OF LEFT. Confirm geometric precision."

STEP 9 — RIGHT FLANKING TOWER (second from right):
Exact geometric bilateral mirror of the Left Flanking Tower. The single horizontal shelf line is at the same proportional height as on the left tower. The upper zone is open shelving; the lower zone is a closed door panel ('?') with the same centered pull mark. Label: "RIGHT TOWER — MIRROR OF LEFT." Include the same advisory annotation about the three-zone alternative.

STEP 10 — THE CONSISTENT HORIZONTAL DATUM — FULL-WIDTH LOWER ZONE BOUNDARY:
Draw or confirm a clear thin horizontal datum line running the full width of the unit at the bridge shelf height level, as an alignment reference. This line visually connects: the top of the left tower lower door zone, the top of the left main display lower base area, the top of the center drawer bank, the top of the right main display lower base area, and the top of the right tower lower door zone. This horizontal datum is the architectural organizing line of the lower zone. Label: "LOWER ZONE DATUM — consistent height across all five sections."

STEP 11 — BASE PLINTH (floor level, full width):
At the very base of the unit, spanning the full width from the outer edge of the left pilaster base block to the outer edge of the right pilaster base block, draw a shallow horizontal band. This band sits between the finished floor line at the very bottom and the base of the lowest cabinet element (the bottom of all lower zone doors and panels). The plinth band is slightly shallower than any drawer or door height. Draw a thin horizontal reveal line along the top edge of the plinth (indicating the slight set-back of the plinth face from the cabinet main face plane). Label: "BASE PLINTH (full width, slightly set back from face plane)."

STEP 12 — GROUND LINE:
At the very bottom of the drawing, draw a clean thin horizontal line running the full drawing width at floor level. Label: "F.F.L. — FINISHED FLOOR LEVEL."

STEP 13 — CENTER AXIS AND SYMMETRY:
Draw a thin vertical dashed line running the complete height of the drawing at the precise horizontal center — the bilateral symmetry axis of the entire composition. Label: "CENTER LINE — BILATERAL SYMMETRY AXIS — EVERY ELEMENT LEFT IS EXACTLY MIRRORED RIGHT."

STEP 14 — HARDWARE SUMMARY (verify all pulls are shown):
Confirm that pull handles appear at the following locations and nowhere else: (a) one elongated vertical bar pull on each of the two center upper door panels, near and parallel to the center seam — two pulls total in the upper center; (b) one centered wave or bar pull on each of the six lower center drawer fronts — six pulls total; (c) one small centered pull mark on each of the two tower lower door panels; (d) one small centered pull mark on each of the two lower base '?' panels of the main display sections; (e) NO hardware appears on any open shelving or open niche areas.

STEP 15 — ANNOTATION MASTER LIST (all labels in the completed drawing):
Verify that the following labels are legible and correctly placed in the final drawing: "ELEM. A — STEPPED CROWN CORNICE (2–3 tiers, center highest — exact count TBD, confirm with client)"; "ELEM. C — PILASTER STRIP (~6 cm — ONLY CLIENT-CONFIRMED DIM.)" × 2; "X-BLOCK CAPITAL (X-PATTERN)" at top of each pilaster × 2; "X-BLOCK BASE (X-PATTERN)" at bottom of each pilaster × 2; "TRANSOM BAND — LOW CONFIDENCE / PENDING CLIENT CONFIRMATION × 1"; "LEFT TOWER" and "RIGHT TOWER"; "OPEN SHELVES" for upper tower zones; "TOWER LOWER DOOR '?' — PENDING CONF." × 2; "LEFT MAIN DISPLAY — STAGGERED OPEN NICHES" and "RIGHT MAIN DISPLAY — MIRROR OF LEFT"; "LOWER BASE '?' PANEL — PENDING CONF." × 2; "CENTER UPPER — DOUBLE DOOR PANELS (×2) — PLAIN BLANK FACES"; "BRIDGE SHELF / COUNTER — PRIMARY HORIZONTAL DATUM"; "6-DRAWER BANK: 2 COLUMNS × 3 ROWS — CENTER STILE VISIBLE"; "LOWER ZONE DATUM — consistent across all sections"; "BASE PLINTH"; "CENTER LINE — BILATERAL SYMMETRY AXIS"; "F.F.L."; "ALL SURFACES: LIGHT BEIGE PAINTED WOOD — UNIFORM."

STEP 16 — OVERALL VISUAL BALANCE VERIFICATION:
The completed drawing should present a wide, commanding composition with a distinctive non-flat, stepped crown. The two narrow pilaster strips at the extreme left and right with their X-marked capital and base blocks create strong architectural vertical terminations. The stepped cornice rises impressively and symmetrically from both outer ends up to the center peak. The upper interior zone is dominated on both flanks by the irregular, organic staggered niche grids — the piece's primary textural event — and anchored in the center by two tall, featureless, plain door panels. The bridge shelf creates a clear, bold horizontal break at counter height in the center. The lower zone reads as a unified horizontal band of closed storage panels and drawer fronts across the full width. The transom band, if rendered, sits as a shallow frieze just below the cornice crown.

CONCLUSION: The completed front elevation is a wide, strictly bilaterally symmetrical architectural cabinet drawing dominated by a multi-tiered stepped crown cornice rising to a center peak (Element A), narrow ornamental pilaster strips with X-pattern capital and base blocks at both outer edges (Element C), an interior upper zone alternating between irregular staggered open niche grids on the flanks and tall plain double door panels at the center, a prominent bridge shelf marking the counter-height datum, a six-drawer bank in two columns and three rows below in the center, closed lower panels across the full lower zone width, a shallow transom band below the cornice annotated as pending client confirmation, and a full-width base plinth at floor — all in uniform light beige painted wood with absolute bilateral symmetry.

generation_notes:
CRITICAL INSTRUCTIONS FOR FAITHFUL REPRODUCTION — every item below must be honored in every generated view:

1. SYMMETRY IS ABSOLUTE: Non-negotiable bilateral mirror around the center vertical axis. Every shelf height, niche width, compartment boundary, door, drawer, and hardware location on the left has a geometrically exact mirror on the right. Even minor asymmetries destroy the fundamental design intent.

2. ELEMENT A — STEPPED CROWN CORNICE: The top of the unit is NEVER a flat horizontal line. It rises in two to three distinct horizontal step tiers from the outer edge (lowest, at each tower crown) toward the center (highest, at the unit's center peak). Each step has a horizontal top face AND a vertical riser face — both visible in front elevation. Step direction: outer = lowest; center = highest. This is the client's primary aesthetic requirement for the top of the piece and must be rendered prominently and correctly in every view.

3. ELEMENT C — OUTER TOWER PILASTER STRIPS (the ONLY client-stated dimension: approximately 6 cm wide): At the OUTERMOST front edge of EACH flanking tower — a narrow vertical strip approximately 6 cm wide with a SQUARE ORNAMENTAL BLOCK bearing X or cross-pattern marks (diagonal crosshatch lines) at its VERY TOP (capital motif) AND at its VERY BOTTOM (base motif). The shaft between the two blocks is plain and flat. Must appear on BOTH towers. Do NOT render these as simple bold border lines or generic side walls — they are deliberate, ornamental front-face pilasters.

4. STAGGERED OPEN NICHE GRID — LEFT AND RIGHT MAIN SECTIONS: The upper areas of both main display sections contain open compartments whose column widths are DELIBERATELY UNEQUAL — some narrow, some wider, in a non-uniform arrangement. Do NOT render as a uniform evenly-spaced grid. Approximately three columns of unequal width × two to three staggered rows per column (shelf heights do not align across columns). The organic, puzzle-like irregularity of this grid is a primary design feature of the piece. Add faint depth lines inside each open compartment to suggest the interior void recedes into the cabinet.

5. CENTER UPPER — TWO TALL PLAIN DOOR PANELS [HIGH CONFIDENCE — all three council members agreed]: Two tall hinged door panels occupying the full upper zone of the center section. Plain, blank faces — NO internal lines, NO shelves, NO subdivisions. Divided by a thin center vertical seam. Each panel bears one elongated vertical bar pull near the center seam. The absolute blankness of these panels is intentional and is in strong contrast with the detailed flanking niche grids.

6. CENTER LOWER — SIX DRAWERS IN TWO-COLUMN × THREE-ROW ARRANGEMENT [HIGH CONFIDENCE — all three council members agreed]: Exactly six drawer fronts: a LEFT COLUMN of three stacked fronts plus a RIGHT COLUMN of three stacked fronts, separated by a visible center vertical stile. Each of the six fronts has a centered wave or bar pull handle. DO NOT render as three single full-width drawers — this is a critical and confirmed distinction.

7. BRIDGE SHELF — CENTER SECTION: A clear horizontal element spanning the center section width at approximately counter height, with a visible front edge face indicating physical thickness. Primary horizontal datum of the composition. The same height is the implied boundary between upper and lower zones across the full unit width.

8. TOWER ZONES — EXACTLY TWO PER TOWER [MAJORITY DECISION: Claude and GPT]: Each tower is divided by exactly ONE horizontal shelf line into an upper open shelf zone (upper half to two-thirds of tower height) and a lower closed door panel zone (lower one-third to one-half of tower height). THREE-ZONE ALTERNATIVE (top door + middle open + bottom door, per Gemini) is noted and plausible — annotate in drawing as 'NOTE: 3-zone alternative raised by council — confirm with client.'

9. TRANSOM UPPER BAND [LOW CONFIDENCE — 2 of 3 council members observed it; 1 disputed it]: Include as a shallow band of small closed compartments directly below the cornice, spanning full interior width. Annotate clearly on every drawing as 'TRANSOM BAND — PENDING CLIENT CONFIRMATION BEFORE FABRICATION.' If absent (per Cloud's reading), the stepped cornice base connects directly to the tops of the niche grids and center door panels.

10. DEPTH CONFIGURATION [UNRESOLVED — CHAIRMAN CALL]: Render with UNIFORM DEPTH throughout as the default interpretation. Gemini's U-shaped stepped-depth plan (upper center section recessed behind towers and base) is architecturally plausible and raised as a serious option, but was not confirmed by a council majority. Annotate the floor plan: 'DEFAULT: UNIFORM DEPTH. U-SHAPED STEPPED DEPTH (upper center recessed) is a plausible alternative — confirm with client before fabrication.'

11. OVERALL DIMENSIONS — RULE: Include NO specific numerical dimensions in any drawing except Element C (approximately 6 cm, the only client-stated value). All other proportions must be rendered and described relatively. No estimated widths, heights, or depths may appear as if they are confirmed measurements.

12. ALL QUESTION-MARK ZONES: Render all '?'-marked areas (tower lower doors, flanking lower cabinet panels, and lower base areas of main display sections) as plain closed panels consistent with the closed lower-storage theme. Annotate each: 'PENDING CLIENT CONFIRMATION.'

13. MATERIAL UNIFORMITY — NO EXCEPTIONS: ALL surfaces throughout — exterior carcass panels, interior shelf faces, open niche side walls and back walls, cornice step faces, pilaster strips, drawer fronts, door panels, transom band, plinth face — are uniform smooth light beige opaque painted wood. Zero contrast zones. Zero exposed wood grain. Zero raw MDF edges.

14. ELEMENT C × ELEMENT A CORNER JUNCTION: The X-block capital of each pilaster strip aligns with and visually connects to the lowest step tier of the stepped cornice at each outer tower corner. Render this junction with care — it is the architectural joint between the two most important trim elements of the piece.
